# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Blackberry (2023) is a phenomenal film and did not get the attention or recognition it deserved

**Score:** 138
**Body:** I saw Blackberry when it came out and I was floored by how engaging and exciting the screen play was and how captivating Glenn Howertons portrayal of Jim Balsille was. I was shocked when it got snubbed during award season and I still dont know what the reasoning was. I have always been an Its Always Sunny Fan and I really thought this was going to be the film that would help Glenn be taken more seriously as an actor and get him more roles. It really feels like he has been overlooked his whole career outside of Sunny and I thought this movie would change things. If you havent seen the movie I highly reccomend it, and let me know what you guys think. Do you think his performance was atleast worthy of some type of nomination? Or am I just a major Glenn Howerton fan? If you have seen it let me know what you think and if you havent go check it out!!!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Matt Johnson is a rising star in the film world. Don't sleep on Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie, it's the most unique take on hidden camera filmmaking I've seen by far. He is also directing the Anthony Bourdain movie coming out later this year, and will be directing a Magic the Gathering movie. 
  - I am so excited for the Bourdain biopic it looks phenomenal !
2. Trailer looks like exactly my kind of thing. On the list thank you 
  - Aye man always feels good recomending films to other film lovers. Let me know what you think!
3. Loved this. Jay Baruchel and Glenn Howerton both deliver fantastic performances full of so much pathos and nuance in addition to their obvious comedic talents. Yeah this is a great movie I'd recommend to anyone. Also, it's Matt Johnson, who is really blowing up now after the success of Nirvanna the Band the Movie. Also check out Johnson's first film The Dirties for an awesome found footage sort of quasi Columbine/school shooter chiller. He's a great filmmaker.
4. Unlimited free texting, only on blackberry...Fuck  Yes. 
5. This movie was so freaking good I hate that no one has seen it
  - I am guessing that it is going to become a cult classic down the road. I can feel it
6. I thought Glenn was great but it's not necessarily an Awards-type performance. Wasn't surprised at all that it didn't get awards recognition. People online definitely loved it though, social media talked a lot about him and the movie
7. I’ve seen it 5 times, it’s one of my personal favs. Glenn Howerton deserved a best actor nomination.

## 2. Movies like - Gattaca, Cosmos, Contact , Comet , Interstellar etc

**Score:** 47
**Body:** Just watched Gattaca and I’m honestly blown away.

The atmosphere, existential themes, human ambition, genetics, destiny vs free will, quiet emotional depth — it felt intellectually beautiful in a way very few films do.

I also loved:

Cosmos

Contact

Comet

Interstellar 

Now I’m looking for more movies or series with that same feeling

Could be slow-burn, cerebral, psychological, space-related, or near-future sci-fi.

What would you recommend that gave you a similar feeling after watching Gattaca or Contact?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival
2. Ad Astra. Has a few missteps, but it’s really good.
3. Moon

Children of Men
4. Solaris
5. Arrival is the peak of this tone and genre.
6. Blade Runner  
Sphere  
Moon  
The Abyss
7. Project Hail Mary.
8. Project Hail Mary was pretty solid.

More of a feel-good space movie. 

I enjoyed it a lot
9. Sunshine

Gravity

Dark City

The Fountain 
10. Blade Runner 2049

## 3. Fun Campy movies/classics

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I have realized that I enjoy really fun Campy movies. Looking for some more suggestions. Ones I have enjoyed recently are Death becomes her, Serial Mom, what ever happened to Baby Jane, Hairspray, Romy and Michelle's high school reunion, etc. Thanks in advance. 
*Edit* Thank-you so much to everyone that has left suggestions. Looking forward to checking these out. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Birdcage
2. But I'm A Cheerleader (1999)

Cry-Baby (1990)
3. If you’re into horror I suggest Tucker & Dale vs. Evil! One of my all timers
4. RV w/Robin Williams 👍
5. Troop Beverly Hills. 
6. The Rocky Horror Picture Show

Jawbreaker

Heathers
7. Psycho Beach Party for intentionally funny camp, Mommy Dearest for unintentionally funny camp.
8. Dude, Where's My Car?

Mars Attacks

Anchorman

Austin Powers

 
9. Fifth Element, Romancing the Stone.
10. Night of the Comet (1983)

## 4. Best Courtroom movies, maybe some less well known

**Score:** 17
**Body:** 12 Angry Men is one of my favorite films of all time and I am hoping to find some more courtroom/trial-adjacent movies that could possibly hold a candle

  
Others that I have watched fairly recently that I enjoyed in case it will help with recs

A Few Good Men - 10/10

Primal Fear

The Rainmaker

Erin Brockovich

The Lincoln Lawyer

My Cousin Vinny - 10/10

A Time to Kill

The Trial of the Chicago 7

Juror #2

  
I feel like I have hit all the ones commonly suggested, so maybe there are some lesser known, cult classics, or underated that have missed

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Witness for the Prosecution.
To Kill a Mockingbird.
Anatomy of a Murder.
Inherit the Wind.

2. Anatomy of a Murder (1959)

The Verdict (1982)

Philadelphia (1993)

The Sweet Hereafter (1997)

Amistad (1997)
  - Another vote for The Verdict (1982).  Great performances by Paul Newman and Charlotte Rampling.
3. Legally Blonde
4. Presumed Innocent &  The Devils Advocate are two of my favorites!
5. Runaway Jury and The Client 
6. Absence of Malice and/or The Verdict - both Paul Newman
7. *...And Justice For All*, with Al Pacino
8. “Philadelphia” (1993) starring *Tom Hanks, Denzel Washington and Antonio Banderas* is a 2x Oscar winner.

“The Pelican Brief” (1993) starring *Julia Roberts, Denzel Washington and Stanley Tucci.*

“Runaway Jury” (2003) starring *Gene Hackman, John Cusack and Dustin Hoffman.*

“Breaker Morant” (1980) starring *Edward Woodward, Bryan Brown and Jack Thompson.*

“In the Name of The Father” (1993) starring *Daniel Day Lewis, Emma Thompson and the wonderful Pete Postlethwaite.*
9. You forgot “My Cousin Vinnie”!

## 5. Movies about quiet love enduring through hardships & passage of time

**Score:** 15
**Body:** So movies where the love angle is not overplayed and not too dramatic.

So some of the movies i really like which have this:

\- brooklyn (the romance between the main characters feels very realistic)

\- The Men (the movie with Marlon brando, not the horror movie)

\- before midnight (the ending is very beautiful)

\- the opening of Up

\- Wall E (yes its animated but its very lovely)

\- the romantic subplot of Conjuring 2 

\- Julie & Julia (Meryl streep-stanely tuccis marriage)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Are you looking for just romance? Or human connection love? 
  - Romance
2. Little Fish (2020)
3. Where the wind blows 
4. I Love You Phillip Morris (2009)
5. One Day
6. Romantic:
- My Year of Dicks (2022)
- When the wind blows (1986)
- Kinsey (2004)

Familial:
- Beasts of the southern wild (2012)
- Thelma (2024)
- Polite society (2023)

Platonic:
- On the count of three (2021)
- Theater Camp (2023)
- Tangerine (2015)
7. The lake house 
8. The Unbearable Lightness of Being.
9. Wings of Desire romantic in a very spiritual and quiet way

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are your thoughts on Karl Urban?

**Score:** 2457

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Diabolical ain't e
2. Fantastic actor.

DREDD was super underrated.
3. When Karl Urban has long hair, it’s fantasy. When Karl Urban has short hair, it’s Sci-Fi. Love how that checks out in most instances. 
  - He has both in that movie with Vin Diesel. Or at least shaved and then a long pony tail. 
    - Exactly. And the Chronicles of Riddick had both elements - sci-fi and fantasy. 

It was supposed to be sci-fi only but when Urban showed up to set with that haircut they rewrote the whole fucking movie on the spot.
4. Oiiii cunttt omelander done take Rebecca 
  - He dun killed me wife n took me bloody son 🗣️🗣️
5.  I dressed as butcher once for Halloween and Karl liked the post, so I’m a bit biased when I say he seems like a neat dude. 
6. Interesting fact: His real name is Charles Rural. 
  - That's an Urban legend

## 2. Having a pretty strong and commanding presence in whatever role she takes on, and being consistently great in her performances, Lena Headey is absolutely in the top league of actors. What’s your opinion on her?

**Score:** 663

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|Q5pSjtPs9wtb2rkLEf)
  - https://preview.redd.it/ehk01q40hi0h1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2c2838b334d7d77e0f48d30732535586cf9b7b87
2. https://preview.redd.it/lyewovn3hi0h1.jpeg?width=615&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5422af2025cbc5af65ae8e5ea47e7d3f8f74ec1f
3. God she was a diabolical bitch in GOT I loved her
4. I honestly think Game of Thrones collapsing near the end kinda overshadowed just how phenomenal she was throughout the series. Cersei could’ve easily turned into a cartoon villain in lesser hands, but Lena Headey made her feel human, bitter, intelligent, pathetic, cruel and weirdly sympathetic all at once. that’s a ridiculously hard balance to pull off
5. She needs to be in more stuff.
6. She's excellent. It's telling that hers is the only Terminator stuff outside of the first two movies that I really like. Plus she's genuinely chilling in Dredd.
  - Terminator: The Sarah Connor Chronicles isn't talked about enough
7. She's good. I wouldn't put her in the top echelon however. 
8. Agreed she’s outstanding and always delivers incredible performances. She can do so much with her face, like silent film queens! 

Oh and she’s received 5 Emmy Award nominations. Can we stop calling every actor who isn’t Meryl Streep or doesn’t have Sandra Bullock’s box office receipts “underrated” please? 

Lena has had a fantastic career. Since “Game of Thrones” she’s appeared in starring roles in 3 television shows for HBO, Paramount and Netflix. She also has another show coming up with Netflix. She’s also directed and received a BAFTA nomination. 

She also has regular work and enjoyed some time off. Lena is doing fine. This “never gets the recognition/underrated” mentality online is exhausting. 

## 3. Happy 62nd birthday to Tim Blake Nelson!! What’s your favorite role from his filmography?

**Score:** 574
**Body:** I’ll start. Mr. Pendanski in Holes. Man, I watched that movie in 6th grade.

I mean, he’s a fiiiine looking man obviously but maaaan, he did something to me the whole time he was on screen in this film, even though it is such a tragic, heartbreaking one. This look of his is breathtaking imo.

## 5. I justo watched Apocalypto

**Score:** 330
**Body:** &#x200B;

The tension never really stops, the cinematography still looks amazing, and the entire chase sequence keeps you glued to the screen. I also feel like very few movies make you feel so immersed in their world like this one does.

I know some people criticize its historical accuracy, but as a cinematic experience, I think it’s incredible.

What do you think? Masterpiece or overrated? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Masterpiece. 
  - Say what you want about Mel Gibson, but the guy is a bonafide genius when it comes to writing and Directing films.
    - What was the South Park quote, "Say what you want about Mel Gibson, but damn it the man knows story structure." 
    - The key and peele skit sums it up perfectly 😂😂

https://youtu.be/CgQk\_WiXVf4?si=THthwG3UTogRifOf
2. https://i.redd.it/lkfq4thuqj0h1.gif

Masterpiece. Jaguar Paw is a badass
  - Almost...
3. It’s an excellent film.  Also Gibson cast multiple indigenous actors, so hell yeah.  
4. This is a film, much like Green Room or Mad Max: Fury Road, that never lets up. It just rips. I watched it maybe 3-4 years ago and I've been trying to forget as much as possible so when i watch it again it feels fresh. I want that initial viewing experience again, hahaha
5. I worked at a movie theater when this came out. On the Wednesday/Thursday before a movie would come out we built the movies (they used to come on reels and had to splice them together on to a larger platter) and we had to ensure there were no issues with the splices so the movie had to be played through completely at least once before Friday. Sometimes I would just let the movie run through and I would check it periodically to make sure the splices didn’t jump, but most of the time employees would want to watch the movies after close if something good was coming out. 

One manager wanted to watch Apocalypto which I had heard nothing about so I sat in the theater and watched it with her. Being a 16 year old kid who loved history, this movie kicked ass. I have not watched it in full since that first viewing but I remember good things!
6. Nothing quite like it. Just an awesome fucking flick.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Spectacle vs subjectivity: Poor Things and The Bride! on womanhood

**Score:** 115
**Body:** I’ve been turning over Poor Things in my head for a while and I keep getting more bothered by it, especially after seeing The Bride!.

Poor Things is widely discussed as feminist, but to me it feels like a very male version of feminism: Bella’s “freedom” is almost entirely sexual, routed through sex, sex work, and a kind of quirky, consequence free nudity. The film claims to be about a woman’s experience but avoids most of the thresholds and banal horrors of womanhood. The camera and narrative return obsessively to her as a sexual object, and the brothel arc in particular sits strangely close to a fantasy of exploitation as empowerment.

Then I watched The Bride! and it felt like I’d been handed the inside of a woman’s head instead of an idea of womanhood curated for male pleasure. Even in the experiment scenes, the way her body is framed is different: they cover her chest, the surface level SA is treated as horrifying rather than sexy, and nudity is not used as part of these two scenes. The movie felt like it was made for me as a woman: it’s about chaos; about what it’s like to be assembled out of other people’s expectations and then told to be grateful for it.

The Bride! leans into the contradiction of these expectations. The world wants her to be creation and abomination, monster and angel, innocent and seducer, muse and threat, fragile and indestructible, an object and an author of her own story. That’s how womanhood has often felt to me: a constant demand to embody opposing roles at once (Madonna/whore, victim/criminal, too much/not enough, desirable but pure, loud but obedient, confident but not 'too much, sexual but not 'slutty,' a victim but never inconvennient), and then be punished for whichever side you land on in the moment. The film lets that female rage spill out rather than smoothing it into something palatable. 

Side by side, I keep wondering: when a “female liberation” story by male creators is so focused on a woman’s sexual availability, is that feminism or a refined male fantasy? Is Poor Things evenreally critiquing the male gaze, or mostly indulging it under the shield of “she’s choosing it”? And does The Bride!’s chaotic rage actually feel more feminist to you, or does it ever feel like the film is confusing female rage with depth?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is actually a conversation I had with my partner after we saw The Bride! a few months ago. I mean, it’s impossible not to compare the two given their premises, and with Emma Stone and Jessie Buckley each lined up for the Oscar their year. But of course, The Bride! was intentionally incoherent, split along all the oppositions you named, and also absurdly blunt in its message (“I’m revolting!” (Dead and disgusting) -> “I’m revolting!” (Revolution)). I can try to frame that positively, as a feminist move, e.g. Maggie Gyllenhaal saying screw the haters, embracing illegibility, letting the entire film become one long scream. But I did also find it baggy, sometimes just lost, scenes that felt really rote or empty (e.g. anything with Penelope Cruz’s detective character, though it’s obvious why this film would include a female detective who needs a man to take credit for her work to be taken seriously, I mean the scenes themselves are just airless). It threw so many strange choices at the wall, like Buckley scatting a thesaurus every few minutes to show she’s also got a Writer, the Mary Shelley, in her skull, which is a funny bit but also exhausting. 

Poor Things does present itself as a kind of feminist fable, but with Lanthimos, it’s impossible to take any of his “moral fables” straight on—this year’s Bugonia, or Killing of a Sacred Deer, for example—and he is interested particularly it seems in naïveté and sexuality and violence, as in Dogtooth. All that said, it’s also too long and meandering, and seems to get a lot of critical mileage out of its production design and performances. I think he’s got a lot better movies, but it makes sense why Poor Things blew up so much, coming in after his collaboration with Emma Stone became cemented, and the feminist fable element is there clearly on the surface for viewers who don’t want to think much deeper than Bella’s sexual “freedom”, and satire is available for those who do. 

All said, I appreciate what Gyllenhaal went for with The Bride!. It’s a crazy, grandiose movie to follow on The Lost Daughter, and while it didn’t actually work for me, I left that theater feeling 0% icky about it, and glad she got the opportunity to go bonkers on screen, and hope she gets to again.
2. I guess one can say that Poor Things investigated (and satirically critiqued) the "born sexy yesterday" trope. And in presenting it (in an entertainment form) perpetuates it. But in presenting it, also opened a path way toward its critique in spaces that would not have it otherwise. It's one of those swords cutting in two directions issues.

I'm not sure how effective "which film is more feminist" purity tests can be here, though you bring up good points. What is important is that you as a woman felt much more affirmed in one and not at all in the other.

I kind of see the point of Poor Things much more of a comparison to the (male) Frankenstein mythos. It isn't a "bride" movie so to speak, so it is looking at different things.

on a sidenote I'd say: wow, two amazing female performances anchoring both films.
  - Just wanted to say I appreciated your respectful comment validating OPs perspective even if you didn't agree with it. I have no opinion on this since I watched neither film but so many filmbros can come across super invalidating or dismissive in general when there's gender issues, feminist themes or even female characters. Thank you for nuanced engagement 
3. I don’t see Poor Things as a “feminist film” - although there are feminist elements. It is more about asserting one’s free and independent consciousness in defiance of oppressive social norms and expectations.

Through the bizarre science fiction set pieces, the film portrays a person whose cognitive development rapidly outpaces the ability for society to indoctrinate her. Normal people undergo nearly two decades of childhood in which our minds absorb uncritically everything around us until it becomes synthesised into a stable worldview inherited from our elders. In Poor Things, though possessing the mind of a child, she maintains the critical intellect of an adult, and the film is about viewing the world through that lens. 

She discovers sex and sexuality the way a normal child might, and is chastised in a normal way that you might expect a normal child to react with internalised shame. Bella, however, reacts critically - she questions why she ought to feel shame for this, and then proceeds to explore this part of the world (as well as everything else) with this same critical authenticity. 

So I see the film about questioning everything we take for granted in social norms and social order, and reconstructing those norms from the perspective of childlike wonder, curiosity, and open mindedness. Feminism is relevant, but it’s more of a footnote than a central thesis of the film. In my opinion. 
  - > So I see the film about questioning everything we take for granted in social norms and social order,

In other words, it's a Yorgos Lanthimos film.

I jest but this was genuinely an excellent comment.
4. > when a “female liberation” story by male creators is so focused on a woman’s sexual availability, is that feminism or a refined male fantasy?  

I'd say it's a third category: a movie that's critical of patriarchy, without pretending it *wasn't* made by a man (and thus isn't remotely immune from the male gaze).  

I think Alex Garland's movies Ex Machina & Men are in the same category: more anti-man than pro-woman. If a feminist male filmmaker wants to engage with sexual politics, I think this is a fine way to do it. (Way way way better than whatever the fuck Zack Snyder thinks he was doing in Sucker Punch - *that* is a sort of deranged fantasy re: its sexual politics.)
  - That Mark Ruffalo's character is a complete buffoon I think has to be incorporated in any reading of the film. It's a very big point being made. In some ways he's a send up of the entire Victorian/Gothic, Austen/Bronte picture of a man breaking you out of your confined situation. credit: a point my wife made, in the side conversation we are having about this thread. (I also took Ruffalo's character to be critically representing some aspects of Capitalism).
    - I love how Ruffalo's character is is such a fake libertine and the movie gradually exposes him as such. Bella is the actual libertine, while he's a conventional, dull hypocrite who ultimately can't handle her or handle her not needing him and leaving him.
      - So funny. Truly one of Ruffalo's greatest performances. I didn't know he had that in him. His arc in this is brilliant and cutting.
5. Completely agreed on Poor Things. It's convenient how a feminism centered around sexual liberation just happens to align with male fantasy. 

The movie started off charming but I quickly soured on it. It's clearly a man's reductive idea of feminism and it was embarrassing how much critical media gave it praise.

I haven't seen The Bride yet, but you've got me interested now.

## 2. Lars Von Trier's unique approach and other filmmakers like him

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Rewatching a few Lars Von Trier films these months (Antichrist being my favorite, but I like most of them), I've come to realize what I find really special and unique about his output is, aside from his great ability to evoke emotions, his particular method of blending classic tragedy and modern provocations. On one hand, he's deeply influenced by filmmakers like Dreyer, Bergman and even some classic theater, and his stories usually unfold in a similar way (with a couple exceptions); on the other hand, he's obviously an iconoclast who not only has a rebellious attitude towards standard filmmaking in favor of alternative means like handheld camera and shooting digital, but he also often employs some strong forms of sex and violence that are usually associated with exploitation films rather than classic arthouse.

Now, I know filmmakers who take from classic tragedies and create something in that vein; I also know filmmakers who have a penchant for extreme content and modern stylistic choices. However, I feel like Lars Von Trier's output might be a perfect midpoint between the two approaches, and I can't think of any other director quite like that.

As the cherry on top, the fact that most of his movies (especially the more extreme ones) are a sort of therapy session for him make them feel more authentic and emotionally powerful. Mind you, I'm quite the big exploitation film defender and I can certainly appreciate some sex and violence for the sake of it, but the realness just adds a certain something to the whole sensory experience.

Aside from that though, I'm just interested to see if you can point to any other filmmaker who blends classic tragedy and modern provocations to quite the same degree, not obviously with the same exact style of course (some of Lars Von Trier's stylistic choices are very much rooted in 90s Danish cinema), just the general idea of it.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the closest living director to that Von Trier midpoint is probably Philippe Grandrieux, especially with Sombre (1998) and La Vie Nouvelle (2002)—he fuses classic tragic structures (predatory innocence, doomed love) with sensory onslaught, raw digital cinematography, and truly transgressive violence and sexuality, all filtered through a deeply subjective, almost feverish form. Catherine Breillat is another—Fat Girl (2001) and Anatomy of Hell (2004) are both heavily existential, almost Greek tragedies at heart, but their clinical sexuality and narrative provocations veer sharply into modern discomfort. And maybe Cristi Puiu—Aurora (2010) especially—takes the dogme/digital discipline and leads it somewhere relentlessly bleak in its portrait of alienation and violence, classic in structure but deeply modern in its execution and ethical challenge.
  - Grandrieux is nothing like von Trier.
    - Agreed. Love Grandrieux, but his work is way more high-brow abstract artsy than Von Trier. Breillat is an apt comparison, but I think Haneke is probably the most similar.
      - I think Haneke is pretty close regarding matter but von Trier's films, despite their dark subjects can be pretty melodramatic and or funny. I think a lot of it comes from von Trier understanding and wanting to seduce an audience with images regardless if he's experimenting or shooting something conventionally. Something like Nymphomaniac could've been made a hundred times more boring or sterile but LVT really nailed it.

Haneke is a bit heavier to me and is perhaps a little more interested in implicating the viewer, but equally rewarding!
      - I think Haneke is very different in the sense that he's a lot more moralizing (for better or worse) than Von Trier is, and he shoots his movies in a much colder style. That being said, The Piano Teacher specifically does strike a similar emotional chord to many Von Trier films so your comparison is not completely off-base. 
        - I just re-watched The Piano Teacher a few days ago lol. Have you seen much Fassbinder? I think his work is probably the most influential on Trier of all.
2. Fassbinder is probably the best answer here. 

In terms of using the film/script as a way exploring his personal issues: in a year with 13 moons , Fox and his friends, and Merchant of Four Seasons stand out

Fassbinder was very influenced by Sirk, Early Goddard and it shows up in a lot of his films.

The most stylized ones: In a year with 13 moons, Lola, Gods of the Plague, Querelle , Chinese Roulette and Despair.


He was also very interested in theatre, Bretcht and contemporary German theatre.
He wrote plays.

The most  indebted to theatre, tragic; Merchant of Four Seasons, ali fear eats the soul, fear of fear, the tears of Petra.
3. I wouldn't say they're exactly similar, but fellow dane Thomas Vinterberg is probably worth watching if you haven't seen his stuff yet. The Celebration is one of the earliest Dogme 95 movies so it shares a lot in common with early von Trier, and The Hunt and Another Round are great too (the latter quite a bit lighter in tone, which can be a relief).

Ruben Ostlund is another modern provacateur that I think operates on a similar wavelength. A bit far removed from "classical tragedy" but I think there are some roots of that in the scripts if you kind of dig for it.

## 3. Night and Fog in Japan (1960)

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Director- Oshima Nagisa.

This is the third time I have seen this film. The first time I saw it, I thought it was pretty good, but I couldn't really understand the film. The second time, I had just started understanding the charm of the New Wave and found it a lot more accessible. This time around, I found it very inaccessible paradoxically due to a lack of understanding of the varying ideologies the two groups represented. I don't think the groups can be completely clustered into being reduced to the 50s Communists and the 60s Protestors, as it takes away a lot of their specific identities and viewpoints. The film requires the viewers to do a good chunk of homework before watching it to be able to understand each event taking place and what they represent/lead to.

The first time I saw it, I thought that the marriage represented the waning youth of the radicals, with them entering adulthood and losing their will to join the front lines and with it their urgency to change the world above all else. The second time I watched it, I thought that the marriage acts as a Synthesis between the Thesis, the 50s Communists and the Antithesis, the 60s Protestors, making it a marriage of two ideologies. This time around, I see it as a warning for the future, endless discourse about the glory days of the past and the inactivity in the present, which causes an endless debate leading to nowhere and making no changes to society. The characters continuously revisit old betrayals, failures, and ideological disputes, yet nothing materially changes, with the result being an endless cycle of political performance that generates discussion without transformation.

In many ways, the film mirrors Heroic Purgatory by Yoshida Yoshishige, the only difference being the nihilism of Yoshida after the failure of the New Left of the 60s, as compared to the optimism of Oshima at the start of the New Left. Both films are a critique of flawed leadership, how dissenting opinions are curbed with numbers and not facts and how having blind faith pushes one straight into the line of fire. Both films weaponise memory, discourse, and repetition to expose the paralysis and fragmentation of political identity itself. The film does not simply critique failed political movements; it critiques the conditions that allow ideological systems to endlessly reproduce their own failures.

The leader of the old left in Night and Fog in Japan says a lot of things, but they mean nothing. He is an opportunist unable to change himself, unable to move on with the times and see their present as what it is, a glaring example of their failure. His words are less about communication and more about preserving what little self worth he attributes to himself. Even when the film ended, his monologue was still going on and on, showing that nothing had changed. Along with this, he makes use of Stalinist rhetoric extensively as a means of constantly positioning himself in a superior position to the rest.

The film is a great example of how history keeps repeating, and nothing really changes. Throughout the film, Oshima uses repetition to further push this. Such as, there are two weddings happening, two students got reprimanded, two protests happened, two times the teacher failed to exert his leadership, etc. These repetitions create the sensation that history itself has become structurally incapable of progression, endlessly reproducing variations of the same political failures. The film thereby transforms repetition into a historical condition rather than simply a stylistic choice.

Similar to Heroic Purgatory, several of the youth in Night and Fog in Japan are shown to have inherited the radical ideals as an inherited dogma, no different from the imperial or patriarchal systems that preceded them. This mirrors the state of the Youth radicalism of the late 60s, showing us how the movement was doomed to failure from the start itself. They do not understand the purpose of them or what they represent, choosing to emulate them. They display champagne leftism, which has them emulate the actual Left as a means of finding purpose in their miserable and inconsequential existence. The people who are only emulating the ideologies are vain and performative and are to be blamed for more of the burden of the decline.

The film can even be viewed as Oshima's arrogance or sense of superiority of the previous generation driven by their betrayal towards the 60s New Left. Thus he portrays the 50s Communists as deeply compromised figures whose attachment to institutional hierarchy and ideological orthodoxy transforms revolutionary politics into another form of authoritarian structure. In this way, the film also mirrors The Ceremony if we look at the patriarchal family structure in one to be similar to the authoritarian structure in the other. Both films showcase a never ending cycle of submission across generations through ritual and obligation.

A new thing I came to know with this watch is that the Japanese Communist Party had switched to parliamentary moderation in the 1960s. I was also able to understand more about the background this time around. Oshima is also critiquing the never ending cycle of an authoritarian government. The only way out is to defeat them, but even if we succeed, another such system can rise to take its place. Oshima is not simply criticising revolutionary failure but also examining the institutional absorption and neutralisation of radical movements into bureaucratic political structures. This historical shift contributes heavily to the atmosphere of exhaustion and betrayal permeating the film.

The film’s core messaging for me concerns the terrifying persistence of political structures and the ease with which revolutionary movements become trapped within cycles of repetition, performance, and institutional self-preservation. Oshima seems deeply sceptical of ideological systems that prioritise organisational continuity over genuine self-critique, yet the film never fully descends into hopelessness. Its anger emerges precisely because it still believes political transformation matters. The Ceremony feels like the aftermath of this belief collapsing almost entirely, where repetition no longer produces conflict but only resignation.

Even though the film can feel overwhelmingly dense, inaccessible, and exhausting at times, I think this difficulty is largely intentional. The constant interruptions, overlapping historical references, and unresolved ideological disputes force the viewer into the same condition of paralysis consuming the characters themselves. The film refuses catharsis because catharsis would falsely imply resolution.

Nevertheless, it is precisely within this exhaustion and fragmentation that the film locates its most compelling insights, presenting political failure not as a singular historical event but as an ongoing structural condition that continuously reproduces itself across generations. Oshima transforms memory into a battleground where history remains permanently unfinished, allowing contradiction and ambiguity to persist not as weaknesses of political thought but as unavoidable consequences of collective historical experience.

Overall, this is a great watch and with it has 51st 5/5 for me. Next, I will watch an Ozu film, maybe Tokyo Twilight.
